% Section: Validation on WeightWatcher's canonical cases (LEAD results).
% All numbers are macros from generated_values.tex; figures from paper/figures

\section{Validation on WeightWatcher's Own Canonical Cases}
\label{sec:ww_replication}

Before applying the Bayesian diagnostics to new modalities, we test them where the
answer is already published: on the exact pretrained checkpoints Martin \& Mahoney
used to establish heavy-tailed self-regularization. We load each model from its public
source (torchvision ImageNet weights \citep{simonyan2015vgg}; HuggingFace GPT/GPT2
\citep{radford2019gpt2}), extract every 2D weight matrix with $\min(\text{shape}) \geq
50$, and compute both the frequentist WeightWatcher point estimate and the \texttt{wwjd}
posterior (\cref{tab:ww_summary}). Three findings result.

\paragraph{The ``GPT2 is better trained than GPT'' gap largely dissolves under $x_{\min}$ marginalization.}
The canonical NLP example holds that GPT is poorly trained because it has ``numerous
unusually large $\alpha$,'' while GPT2-small has all $\alpha \leq 6$. Under the
frequentist single-$x_{\min}$ estimate we reproduce this: mean $\alpha = \wwGptFreq$ for
GPT versus $\wwGptTwoFreq$ for GPT2 and $\wwGptTwoMedFreq$ for GPT2-medium. Under the
\texttt{wwjd} posterior that marginalizes over the scaling window, all three collapse
into one healthy band: $\wwGptBayes$, $\wwGptTwoBayes$, and $\wwGptTwoMedBayes$
respectively (\cref{fig:gpt}). The frequentist gap (GPT $-$ GPT2 $= \wwGptGapFreq$)
shrinks $\wwGptGapShrink\times$ to $\wwGptGapBayes$. Per layer, GPT's $\wwGptBigN$
$768\times768$ attention-projection matrices fall from frequentist $\alpha \in
[\wwGptBigAlphaFreqLo, \wwGptBigAlphaFreqHi]$ to posterior $\alpha \in
[\wwGptBigAlphaBayesLo, \wwGptBigAlphaBayesHi]$. The shift is not an estimator artifact:
$\wwGptXminShareLo$--$\wwGptXminShareHi\%$ of the per-layer $\alpha$ variance on these
layers is attributable to the $x_{\min}$-window choice alone (the law-of-total-variance
decomposition of the posterior), exactly the degree of freedom the single KS-optimal
window collapses.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.96\textwidth]{fig_gpt_dissolution.png}
\caption{The GPT/GPT2 ``training quality'' gap is largely a scaling-window artifact.
\textbf{Left:} each point is one weight matrix; the frequentist $\alpha$ (single KS
window) versus the \texttt{wwjd} posterior $\alpha$ (Bayesian model-averaged over
$x_{\min}$). Layers the point estimate calls ``unusually large'' ($\alpha > 5$) collapse
toward $\alpha \approx 3$. \textbf{Right:} mean $\alpha$ per model; the frequentist gap
that underwrites ``GPT is poorly trained'' shrinks $\wwGptGapShrink\times$ under the
posterior.}
\label{fig:gpt}
\end{figure}

Two independent goodness-of-fit tests then contradict the ``not well-described by a power
law'' mechanism on these same layers. The Bayesian model comparison prefers a power law
over both exponential (log Bayes factor $+\wwGptBfExpLo$ to $+\wwGptBfExpHi$) and
lognormal tails, and the posterior-predictive $p$-values are all above $0.05$
($\wwGptPpcLo$--$\wwGptPpcHi$): the power-law fit is adequate in absolute terms. When we
extend the comparison set to the truncated power law---the model WeightWatcher itself
ships because plain power-law fits over-estimate $\alpha$---and the generalized Pareto
(the extreme-value peaks-over-threshold tail), the truncated power law beats the plain one
on all $\wwGptTplWin/\wwGptNlayers$ GPT layers. The ``large $\alpha$'' is therefore a
power-law-\emph{with-truncation} mis-specification, made explicit: when the finite-size
cutoff is modeled, the inflation disappears (\cref{fig:mech}). A
region-of-practical-equivalence test keeps this honest---only $\wwGptRopeN/\wwGptNlayers$
GPT layers place majority posterior mass in $\alpha \in [1.75, 2.25]$, so the Bayesian
$\alpha \approx \wwGptBayes$ is far below the frequentist $\wwGptFreq$ but still above the
RG-ideal $2.0$. A power-scaling prior-sensitivity diagnostic confirms the conclusions are
not prior-driven (mean sensitivity $\wwGptPriorSens$ posterior-SD units).

\paragraph{The VGG weighted-$\alpha$ trend is confirmed---and sharper under the posterior.}
WeightWatcher's flagship claim is that the weighted alpha $\hat\alpha =
\text{mean}_\ell\, \alpha_\ell \log_{10}\lambda_{\max,\ell}$ tracks test accuracy across
the VGG11/13/16/19 ($\pm$BN) ImageNet series. We reproduce this with the frequentist point
estimate (Pearson $r = \wwVggRFreq$) and find the \emph{Bayesian} $\hat\alpha$ tracks
accuracy \emph{more} strongly, $r = \wwVggRBayes$ (\cref{fig:vgg}): the model-averaging
over $x_{\min}$ pulls down the inflated large-$\alpha$ layers, making the metric more
predictive, not less. The metric must be the per-layer mean: the unweighted mean $\alpha$
gives $r = \wwVggRUnweighted$, the wrong sign---a depth-confounded Simpson's paradox
\citep{martin2021postmortem}---which the weighting corrects and the posterior recovers
cleanly. We close the loop with an errors-in-variables regression of accuracy on
$\hat\alpha$ that propagates the per-model $\hat\alpha$ posterior uncertainty
(\cref{sec:method}): the posterior slope is $-6.2$ percentage points of top-1 accuracy per
unit $\hat\alpha$ (95\% credible interval $[-7.8, -4.8]$, $P(\text{slope} < 0) = 1.00$),
residual $\sigma = 0.31$pp---the weights-only spectrum predicts ImageNet top-1 to within a
third of a point. The measurement-error slope is slightly steeper than the naive
ordinary-least-squares slope ($-6.0$), the expected attenuation correction.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\begin{minipage}{0.49\textwidth}\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig_vgg_eiv.png}
\caption{VGG weighted-$\hat\alpha$ as a calibrated accuracy predictor. The
errors-in-variables regression propagates per-model $\hat\alpha$ posterior uncertainty
(horizontal error bars); the band is the $95\%$ predictive interval. The posterior
$\hat\alpha$ tracks accuracy ($r = \wwVggRBayes$) more strongly than the frequentist
($r = \wwVggRFreq$).}
\label{fig:vgg}
\end{minipage}\hfill
\begin{minipage}{0.49\textwidth}\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig_mechanism.png}
\caption{The unifying mechanism. The posterior pull-down ($\alpha_{\rm freq} -
\alpha_{\rm bayes}$) grows with the frequentist $\alpha$ across image and language models:
the single-window estimate inflates $\alpha$ most on the hard-to-fit (ambiguous-$x_{\min}$,
truncated) layers, and the Bayesian treatment corrects it there; clean low-$\alpha$ layers
barely move.}
\label{fig:mech}
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{Batch normalization breaks the power law in a way the point estimate hides.}
Across the BatchNorm VGG variants, the Bayesian model comparison rejects the power law (in
favor of exponential or lognormal) for $\wwBnRejLo$--$\wwBnRejHi\%$ of layers, rising with
depth; plain VGG and all GPT models reject for $0\%$. The frequentist $\alpha$, which never
tests the power-law assumption, is blind to this structure.

\paragraph{$\alpha$ tracks training \emph{maturity}, not data domain.}
The frozen-checkpoint results above compare models trained by others. To isolate what
\emph{training itself} does to the spectrum, we trained GPT-2-124M ourselves on an open
neuroscience corpus (the 1.3B-token BrainGPT PMC subset) in two regimes and compared the
spectra to stock GPT-2 (\cref{fig:maturity}). A \emph{gentle domain fine-tune} (the
pretrained model continued for one epoch, learning rate $2\times10^{-5}$) leaves the
spectrum essentially untouched: mean posterior $\alpha$ moves from $\wwBgBaseBayes$ to
$\wwBgFtBayes$ (mean $|\alpha-2|$ identical at $\wwBgBaseDist$). A model trained
\emph{from scratch} for the same one epoch is markedly less mature: posterior $\alpha =
\wwBgScratchBayes$ (frequentist $\wwBgScratchFreq$), with mean $|\alpha-2| = \wwBgScratchDist$
versus $\wwBgBaseDist$ for the pretrained base---the embedding and early attention/MLP
layers carry the largest gap. A larger $\alpha$ is a \emph{lighter} tail: the heavy-tailed
structure has not yet formed. Training pulls $\alpha$ down toward the RG-optimal $2$, and
adaptation of an already-mature model does not move it---so the spectrum reflects \emph{how
well-trained} a model is rather than \emph{what} it was trained on, a direct controlled
confirmation of the HT-SR maturity claim. (Notably, even the one-epoch from-scratch model is
still power-law-distributed: the Bayes factor rejects the power law for $0\%$ of its layers,
so immaturity manifests as a lighter power-law tail, not as loss of power-law structure.)

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.55\textwidth]{fig_training_maturity.png}
\caption{Controlled training-maturity test on GPT-2-124M we trained ourselves. A one-epoch
neuroscience \emph{fine-tune} of stock GPT-2 leaves mean $\alpha$ unchanged (mature spectrum,
domain adaptation does not reshape it); a one-epoch \emph{from-scratch} model sits well above
the RG optimum $\alpha=2$ (immature: the heavy tail has not formed). $\alpha$ tracks training
maturity, not data domain.}
\label{fig:maturity}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{Watching the heavy tail form: the $\alpha$ trajectory.}
The from-scratch checkpoint above is a single immature snapshot because its learning rate
($2\times10^{-5}$) was held to the fine-tune recipe. To resolve the \emph{dynamics}, we trained
a second GPT-2-124M from scratch with a proper from-scratch schedule (learning rate
$6\times10^{-4}$ cosine, $\beta_2=0.95$, weight decay $0.1$), saving $17$ checkpoints on a
log-spaced schedule from random initialization to step $\wwTrajSteps$ (one epoch), and ran
\texttt{wwjd} on every checkpoint (\cref{fig:traj}). The mean posterior $\alpha$ falls from
$\wwTrajInitBayes$ at random initialization to $\wwTrajMinBayes$ by step $\wwTrajMinStep$---most
of the descent is complete within the first $\sim$15\% of one epoch---then relaxes to
$\wwTrajFinalBayes$, essentially the stock-GPT-2 value ($\wwGptTwoBayes$): with a real learning
rate, one epoch \emph{does} reach maturity. The heavy tail forms layer-by-layer, with structure:
token embeddings start lightest ($\alpha=6.1$, nearest to pure noise) and travel farthest, while
the attention and MLP \emph{output} projections form their heavy tail first, undershooting
$\alpha<2.3$ near step $500$ before relaxing back toward the band. The frequentist single-window
estimate tells the same story far more crudely: it starts at $\wwTrajInitFreq$, because the
random-init Marchenko--Pastur bulk has no power-law tail and so no stable scaling window for a
single KS-optimal $x_{\min}$ to find. The resulting posterior pull-down is $\wwTrajInitPull$ at
initialization and collapses to $\wwTrajFinalPull$ as the spectrum matures (\cref{fig:traj},
right)---the identical mechanism as the frozen-checkpoint plot (\cref{fig:mech}), now resolved in
training time: the single-window estimate is least trustworthy exactly where the scaling window is
least defined, and the Bayesian treatment is most needed there.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.98\textwidth]{fig_alpha_trajectory.png}
\caption{The HT-SR power law forming during training (GPT-2-124M from scratch, lr
$6\times10^{-4}$, one epoch, \texttt{wwjd} on $17$ checkpoints). \textbf{Left:} the Bayesian
$\alpha$ of each layer type descends from the random-init light tail toward the RG optimum
$\alpha=2$; thick line is the mean ($\wwTrajInitBayes\!\to\!\wwTrajFinalBayes$). Token embeddings
travel farthest; output projections mature first. \textbf{Right:} the frequentist single-window
inflation ($\alpha_{\rm freq}-\alpha_{\rm bayes}$) collapses from $\wwTrajInitPull$ at random
initialization to $\wwTrajFinalPull$---the \cref{fig:mech} mechanism in training time.}
\label{fig:traj}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{The heavy tail is the weight-space shadow of a forming circuit.}
A spectral exponent matters only if it corresponds to computation. We tested this directly by
running two mechanistic-interpretability probes on the \emph{same} $17$ checkpoints: the per-head
\emph{induction score} \citep{olsson2022induction} (does a head attend to, and copy, the token
that followed the current token's previous occurrence?) and the OV \emph{copying score}
\citep{elhage2021framework}---the fraction of a head's output--value circuit eigenvalues with
positive real part, an eigenvalue statistic in the same language as $\alpha$. Three facts result
(\cref{fig:circuit}). \textbf{(i)~Heavy-tail formation and circuit formation are locked.} Across the
trajectory the mean posterior $\alpha$ correlates $\wwCircCorrInd$ with the maximum induction score
and $+\wwCircCorrSR$ with the mean weight stable rank ($\lVert W\rVert_F^2/\lVert W\rVert_2^2$): the
HT-SR exponent and the elementary stable rank track the same spectral concentration, and both
anti-track the induction circuit. \textbf{(ii)~The spectrum leads the circuit.} The stable rank
collapses first---the spike separating from the Marchenko--Pastur bulk, reaching $<\!60$ by step
$\wwCircSpikeStep$---and only \emph{then} does an induction head ignite (score $>\!0.1$ at step
$\wwCircIndStep$, $>\!0.4$ at step $\wwCircIndStrong$) while the top singular subspaces freeze
(principal-angle drift $\to 0$). The heavy tail forms, and the computation comes online inside it
$\sim$250--500 steps later. \textbf{(iii)~One circuit, two signatures.} The dominant induction head
(layer~7) acquires its behavioural induction score and its OV copying eigenvalues together, ending
at induction $\wwCircFinalInd$ with $\wwCircNHeads$ induction heads model-wide. This is a controlled,
label-free demonstration that $\alpha$ is not a bystander statistic: the same steps that pull $\alpha$
toward $2$ are the steps that build an interpretable circuit, and the spectral signature slightly
\emph{precedes} the behavioural one---consistent with the heavy tail being the enabling cause rather
than a downstream correlate.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth]{fig_circuit_trajectory.png}
\caption{Mechanistic interpretability paired with heavy-tail formation, on the same from-scratch
GPT-2 checkpoints. \textbf{Left:} the induction circuit ignites (red) just as the Bayesian $\alpha$
(blue) crosses into the heavy-tailed band (shaded: the spike-formation window). \textbf{Center:} the
weight stable rank collapses first (purple, the spike forming) and the top singular subspaces then
freeze (orange, drift~$\to 0$). \textbf{Right:} a single head (L7H1) comes online with both
signatures at once---behavioural induction (red) and its OV copying eigenvalue fraction (cyan; $0.5$
= no copying). $\alpha$ correlates $\wwCircCorrInd$ with induction and $+\wwCircCorrSR$ with stable
rank across the trajectory.}
\label{fig:circuit}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{Can you \emph{steer} $\alpha$? A causal test, and a Goodhart warning.}
The lock between heavy-tail formation and circuit formation invites the obvious intervention: add a
differentiable regularizer that pulls every layer's $\alpha$ toward the RG-optimal $2$ and ask
whether circuits form sooner. We ran the seed-matched controlled version---two from-scratch
GPT-2-124M with \emph{identical} initialization and data order, differing only by the regularizer
(the differentiable Hill-estimator surrogate $\lambda\sum_\ell(\alpha_\ell-2)^2$, $\lambda=1$, on
the same matrices)---and analyzed both with the full pipeline (\cref{fig:areg}). The result is a
clean Goodhart failure. The regularizer drove its \emph{own} (Hill) target to $2$ (penalty
$<\!10^{-5}$), yet the rigorous \texttt{wwjd} posterior $\alpha$ moved the \emph{wrong} way: it ended
\emph{further} from $2$ than the unregularized baseline ($\wwAregRegAlpha$ vs $\wwAregBaseAlpha$), and
diverged most mid-training ($\wwAregRegAlphaMid$ vs $\wwAregBaseAlphaMid$ at step $500$). The spectrum
became \emph{less} concentrated, not more (mean stable rank $\wwAregRegSR$ vs $\wwAregBaseSR$). The
model simply found weights that satisfy the fixed-window Hill metric without a genuinely heavier
tail---and the Bayesian estimator, which marginalizes the window, is exactly what exposes the hack
that the single-window surrogate hides. Crucially, the intervention bought \emph{no circuit}: language
modeling was unchanged (final loss $\wwAregRegLoss$ vs $\wwAregBaseLoss$) and the induction circuit
formed at essentially the same time and strength (max induction $\wwAregRegInd$ vs $\wwAregBaseInd$;
$8$ induction heads either way). Forcing the metric does not build the mechanism. We read this as
direct evidence that $\alpha$ is a \emph{diagnostic readout} of training quality, not a control knob:
the heavy tail that emerges under good training (\cref{fig:traj,fig:circuit}) is downstream of the
computation, and optimizing the exponent directly is a metric-hack the point estimate rewards and the
posterior catches.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth]{fig_alpha_reg_causal.png}
\caption{Causal test of the $\alpha\!\to\!2$ regularizer (seed-matched pair; only the regularizer
differs). \textbf{Left:} the regularizer reaches its Hill target ($\alpha=2$, green) but makes the
\emph{Bayesian} $\alpha$ \emph{worse} (red above blue throughout)---a Goodhart hack the posterior
detects. \textbf{Center:} induction forms about the same in both arms. \textbf{Right:} the regularized
spectrum is \emph{less} concentrated (higher stable rank). $\alpha$ is a readout of training, not a
lever: forcing it neither matures the spectrum (by the rigorous measure) nor builds the circuit.}
\label{fig:areg}
\end{figure}

is the single-$x_{\min}$ WeightWatcher estimate, ``bayes'' the \texttt{wwjd} posterior;
PL-rej.\ is the fraction of layers the Bayes factor rejects as non-power-law.}
